author: @dan1694
text: I do have a question for you Robert.  If you increased the size of the magnets to a greater extent would you be able to increase the distance from the graphite and the magnet to the extent that the magnet could be completely isolated thermally thus extending the lifespan of the magnet utilizing only passive systems or would an active electrically generated magnetic field be necessary?  Thanks,  Daniel

replies.author: @stevecollins4567
replies.text: Surely the load on the field is what degrades the magnet.

author: @dan1694
text: As always an awesome video Robert.  I've been at home recovering from surgery and been following your progress.  Thank you for sharing  your knowledge and passion for science, it's quite refreshing.  I do have a cheap easy idea that might help with this experiment.  Put everything under a vacuum and repeat the experiment as repeated high heat exposure will weaken the magnets.  I'm no expert but the vacuum should prevent thermal transference to some extent and allow extended mechanical rotation.

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: will keep you up to date mate

author: @morphtrust
text:
whoops I forgot the reason it is different from the perspective of the graphite, so here it is
pyrolytic graphite is only diamagnetic in perpendicular direction to the plane of the graphene, along the cleaving plane (think of it for anyone reading here as a book with pages pressed together, so facing the cover of the book back or front, the graphite is very strongly diamagnetic, and along the edges of the pages it is strongly ferromagnetic :) this is how it quantum locks in a magnetic field:)

author: @morphtrust
text: funny thing about bismuth too as opposed to pyrolytic graphite (controlled layers of graphene all going the same direction for the most part) is that it is omni directional in it's output. This is something I am currently working with on a very serious level. :) definitely will be very interested in any other news on your development on this :)

author: @ThinkingandTinkering
text: The heat from the sunlight changes the diamagnetic properties of the graphite and so disturbs the equilibrium of the system. But as the diamagnetic graphite is very very thermally conductive as soon as it moves out of the heat﻿ spot the heat is dissipated across the surface and lost rapidly. This continues and the disk spins.

author: @JustinHallPlus
text:
Are your books in print, or just in electronic version?  I want to buy a couple of them but I don't have an e-reader and reading a book on my monitor would probably lead to many headaches (literally).

I'm going to be building a large solar concentrator out of recycled materials this summer, and I think a lot of your ideas will be good experiments, particularly the one involving separating water into hydrogen and oxygen with Graphitic Carbon Nitride.

Great video as always.

author: @MYNICEEV
text: Would it be possible to keep the graphite stationary and the magnet spin when sunlight was shone on the graphite? I am thinking large ring magnet attached to a shaft. then a round window with a lens inside. As the sun shone down the tube it hit the fixed piece of graphite and the re-action forced the magnet to turn and thereby the shaft. Then connect generator to shaft and get power. Just a thought.
